metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the `apply_failclosed` → `apply_fail_mode` expand-contract story against the actual current state: if `apply_failclosed` exists only in the `b744` worktree (not main-tree), explicitly state that this migration step is post-b744 and remove the 'KEPT as a thin wrapper' language that implies the function already exists in the current codebase.
findings.finding: The plan treats `apply_failclosed` as existing, shippable code ('apply_failclosed(…) is KEPT as a thin wrapper that calls the new apply_fail_mode(…, fail_mode="closed")') and describes an expand-contract migration of its callers — but `apply_failclosed` does not exist anywhere in the main-tree codebase. It lives only in the `b744` worktree (`worktree-code-review-capability`, WS1–WS5 landed there), which the plan itself identifies as a dependency. The 'KEPT as a thin wrapper / rollback = revert callers' story is describing code whose current state in the accessible codebase is non-existent, making the capability assertion unverifiable and the backwards-compatibility guarantee ungroundable.
findings.location: § DET-invariant scan scoping & fail-open (clears COH) — '`apply_failclosed` → `fail_mode` expand-contract (clears T4; story `7f0d`)' sub-section
findings.suggested_fix: Reframe the `apply_failclosed` section to acknowledge the two-phase reality: (1) once b744 lands and `apply_failclosed` / `code_review/detectors.py` are in main-tree, the expand-contract migration proceeds as described; (2) until then, this section describes future state, not current state. Add a dependency note matching the pattern used elsewhere in the plan ('Gated on b744').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `project.<name>` ID namespace conflict with the shared `_valid_id` regex (`^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9-]*$` forbids dots): either choose a dot-free namespace prefix (e.g. `project-<name>`) that validates without code changes, or explicitly plan the regex relaxation and re-verify the path-traversal safety argument for the new character set.
findings.finding: The plan's `project.<name>` criterion ID namespace uses a literal dot (e.g. `project.foo`), but the shared `_valid_id` regex (`^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9-]*$` at `prompt_authoring.py:50`) explicitly forbids dots. Both `/library/create` and `create_prompt` delegate to `_validate_id` → `_valid_id`, so authoring a `project.foo` criterion via the editor's `/library/create` endpoint would be rejected with `InvalidPromptIdError`. The plan says `project.<name>` ids flow through `/library/create` (reuses it), creating a direct collision with the existing shared ID validator.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'a net-new project criterion id MUST be `project.<name>`-prefixed'; § What exists vs. what we build — 'Visual editor extended to author project criteria (reuses `/library/create`)'; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/prompt_authoring.py:50` (_SLUG regex); `src/rebar/llm/prompt_library.py:186-191` (_validate_id delegates to _valid_id)
findings.suggested_fix: Use `project-<name>` (dash, not dot) as the namespace prefix — it passes the existing `_valid_id` regex unchanged, still distinguishes project criteria from built-ins by prefix, and maps to a safe `.rebar/prompts/project-<name>.md` filename. Update the overlay collision rule to: an id starting with `project-` is a new project criterion; an un-prefixed id is a built-in re-tune/disable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RED test for the attestation-invalidation-at-claim-time path: create a cert with no overlay, activate/edit/disable a criterion via `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, then assert `claim_gate_check` treats the prior cert as stale (not merely that routing returns different results).
findings.finding: The attestation-invalidation-at-claim-time code path has no planned RED/probe test. The plan threads `repo_root` through `registry_version → compute_validity → claim_gate_check` (and `drift_refresh_candidate`, `remediation_mode_candidate`), asserts this will invalidate a prior attestation when the overlay changes, but supplies no RED fixture that verifies the claim. This is a distinct path from the cache-isolation RED test (which covers routing leakage, not claim-time invalidation).
findings.location: § Attestation & invalidation / Acceptance Criteria: 'registry_version(repo_root) hashes the overlay; activating/editing/disabling a criterion invalidates a prior attestation at claim time'
findings.suggested_fix: In the build order, add a test fixture after MVP slice step 1: write a test that (a) calls `claim_gate_check` with a valid cert + no overlay → passes; (b) writes a `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` overlay; (c) calls `claim_gate_check` again with the same cert → asserts result is stale/invalid. This directly proves the `registry_version` → invalidation loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to ef7e (or a standalone test story) asserting that a blocking project LLM criterion whose call errors or times out yields skip=PASS (fail-open), mirroring the DET fail-open contract in 7f0d.
findings.finding: The parent AC 'an LLM-block criterion that errors/times out fails open (skipped, does not block)' is not covered by any child's acceptance criteria. Child 7f0d covers DET fail-open explicitly, but no child asserts the analogous LLM error/timeout fail-open behavior for project LLM criteria configured to block.
findings.location: child ef7e-c3ed-296b-4642 (LLM runner path) and child 7f0d-0cb6-16a7-4f26 (DET path only)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ef7e AC: '[ ] An activated project LLM criterion whose LLM call errors or times out does not block (fail-open: skip=PASS); a test injects a call failure on a blocking project criterion and asserts no block is emitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Formalize build-order steps 3 (eval runner + calibration view), 4 (editor live-preview), and 5 (post-b744 code-review unification) as explicit child tickets (or clearly scoped milestones with their own ACs), so each can be reviewed, executed, and closed independently; the current epic ticket should cover only steps 1–2 (MVP slice + DET consumer generalization).
findings.finding: The five build-order steps describe epic-scale scope spanning structurally independent goals (core registry, DET consumer, eval tooling, editor UI, code-review unification) but are presented as a single ticket's sequenced work plan rather than as formal child tickets. Steps 3 (eval runner + calibration view), 4 (editor live-preview + authoring extension), and 5 (code-review unification post-b744) are each independently deliverable and independently valuable, making them candidates for child tickets rather than phases of one ticket.
findings.location: Build order (risk-ordered MVP) section; Acceptance Criteria (9+ ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the sync-vs-async editor preview shape before implementation: either commit to the sync `POST /criterion/preview` shape (with a clear timeout and graceful error response) or specify the async job+poll API contract (endpoint, polling interval, job-state schema) — remove the conditional spike gate and replace it with a decided design.
findings.finding: The editor live-preview endpoint design is deferred to a runtime spike: the plan says 'if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout on the reference model, fall back to an async job+poll shape' — this is a 'verify whether' conditional that leaves the implementation shape (sync vs async) undecided until the executor runs an experiment.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Commit to the simpler sync shape: `POST /criterion/preview` always returns synchronously within the configurable timeout (default 60s); on timeout, return HTTP 504 with `{"error": "timeout", "rationale": "preview timed out"}` rather than falling back to a different API shape. Document that the async path is a follow-on if adoption demands it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the async job+poll fallback protocol for `POST /criterion/preview`: at minimum define the job-id response shape, the poll endpoint URL pattern, in-process vs persisted job state, and the fallback when the async job itself fails/times out.
findings.finding: The `POST /criterion/preview` spike-gate async fallback is referenced but its sub-step protocol is unspecified: the plan says to 'fall back to an async job+poll shape' if the sync preview times out, but never defines the job-state store, the poll endpoint URL, or the failure handling when the async job itself fails or times out.
findings.location: § Net-new / Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `load_criteria` is (a) given a `repo_root` parameter and its `@lru_cache` replaced with a `repo_root`-keyed cache (or removed), or (b) deprecated in favor of `effective_criteria(repo_root)` at all callsites — the current cache-with-no-repo-key is the root cause of the cross-repo leakage and must be addressed explicitly, not just layered over.
findings.finding: `load_criteria()` is `@lru_cache(maxsize=1)` with no `repo_root` cache key, but it internally resolves `repo_root` via `config.repo_root()` inside `_descriptor_from_prompt` (registry.py:145-151). When `effective_criteria(repo_root)` is introduced as an uncached wrapper layered on top, the plan says to route `load_criteria` through every vocabulary callsite — but `load_criteria` itself will still cache the result from whichever `repo_root` was active when first called. In a long-lived MCP server switching between repos, the existing `load_criteria` cache returns the first repo's project overrides to all subsequent callers, which is exactly the cross-repo leakage the plan's cache-isolation RED test is meant to prevent. The plan's fix must also change `load_criteria`'s cache key (or remove it as a direct callsite, replacing it with `effective_criteria(repo_root)` everywhere).
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'Caching (clears G6 cache bug): keep _routing_index() @lru_cache'd for the packaged index (immutable per binary); add effective_routing(repo_root) (uncached, or keyed by (repo_root, overlay mtime)) that merges the overlay; extend prompt_library._invalidate_caches()'; registry.py:126-136 (_routing_index) and 172-190 (load_criteria)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback step for the `registry_version(repo_root=None)` parameter-threading migration: state that the parameter is optional with `None` defaulting to current behavior, that incomplete threading degrades gracefully to non-invalidation (not a crash), and describe the revert procedure (e.g., 'revert the `repo_root` parameter and caller sites as a single commit').
findings.finding: The plan threads `repo_root` through every `registry_version` caller but provides no explicit rollback or revert step for that parameter-threading migration itself — only cert-level expand-contract is described.
findings.location: § Attestation & invalidation — 'thread `repo_root` through **every** caller'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Attestation section, add: '`registry_version` gains `repo_root=None`; callers not yet threaded pass `None` and get the packaged-only hash (safe degradation). Rollback = revert the parameter addition and all threading callsites as one atomic commit; attestations already issued remain valid (stale treatment is expand-contract).' Also add a RED test that asserts the threaded callers all pass a non-None repo_root in a repo with an overlay.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State whether the per-criterion eval runner for `rebar criteria eval <id>` extends `run_case` with a new dispatch branch (and if so, define the `case` dict shape for a plan-review criterion — what fixture fields does it carry?), or introduces a separate runner that invokes the plan-review `eval_specs/` + Inspect-AI seam directly; and confirm that the calibration metrics (recall, false-accept, Cohen's κ) are computed from the same eval output shape as the existing plan-review eval suite.
findings.finding: The `eval_solver.run_case` dispatch is confirmed closed at 3 hardcoded ids (completion-verifier, ticket-quality, spec-alignment; raises ValueError otherwise). The plan's `rebar criteria eval <id>` AC says it 'extends the closed solver dispatch' — but this is a new CLI entrypoint, not just an extension of `run_case`. The plan does not state whether (a) `run_case` itself gains a new `plan-review-criterion` dispatch branch (requiring a `fixture` → synthetic ticket transformation for a plan-review criterion), or (b) a separate `criterion_eval_runner` is introduced that invokes the plan-review gate's own eval machinery (the `eval_specs/` YAML + `rebar.llm.eval` seam) directly, bypassing `run_case`. The two paths differ significantly in architecture: path (a) reuses `run_case`'s disposable store + runner injection but must synthesize a plan-review context; path (b) is more natural (the plan-review eval already has its own `eval_specs/` + Inspect-AI seam) but `run_case` is described as the foundation for the 'per-criterion eval runner' in the plan.
findings.location: § Net-new (build) — 'Per-criterion eval runner — eval_solver.run_case is a closed 3-id dispatch (raises otherwise) + a validate-only CLI; add a criterion-aware runner + a live CLI'; AC 'rebar criteria eval <id> runs a project criterion's must-fire/must-not-fire + discrimination fixtures live'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency on child 7f0d in child 5065's Scope section, and add an AC verifying that the DET descriptor shape built in 7f0d is reused (not re-implemented) in the shared rebar.llm.criteria layer.
findings.finding: Child 5065 declares its shared descriptor as 'exec-tier-polymorphic (LLM overlay + prompt-less DET)' and must consume DET criteria, but 5065 lists only 'BLOCKED ON b744' as its blocker and does not declare a dependency on child 7f0d (which builds the DET descriptor branch and _descriptor_from_prompt DET path). If 7f0d is incomplete or its DET descriptor shape changes when 5065 unifies, 5065's polymorphic descriptor claim is silently broken.
findings.location: child 5065-0192-cc5e-4d01 (Scope / dependency section)
findings.suggested_fix: In 5065's Scope section add: 'Depends on: story #2 (7f0d) for the DET descriptor branch shape; the shared layer reuses the _descriptor_from_prompt DET branch without redefining it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'reference model' explicitly (e.g., name the specific LLM model/tier used as the performance benchmark for the sync-preview spike gate, or remove the spike gate per the suggested fix above).
findings.finding: The 'reference model' used to gate sync-vs-async fallback is undefined — the plan refers to 'the reference model' without specifying which LLM model serves as the performance benchmark for the spike.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the docs deliverable to explicitly cover: (a) the `POST /criterion/preview` endpoint contract (request/response shape, timeout/fallback behavior, required credentials), and (b) the `disabled_builtins` field in the attestation coverage dict (semantics, how it appears in the signed manifest, how cert-parsing tooling should treat absence of the field on old certs).
findings.finding: The new `POST /criterion/preview` editor endpoint is a named API contract but is absent from the documentation deliverable scope, and the new `disabled_builtins` field in the signed attestation coverage dict is also not listed in the docs deliverable.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview'; § Attestation & invalidation — 'disabled_builtins'; § Migration & compatibility — Docs deliverable

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the output schema/format for `rebar criteria eval <id>`'s calibration view: specify field names (e.g. `recall`, `false_accept`, `kappa`, `agreement_pct`), value precision, and rendering format (JSON / table / text) co-located with the AC or the § Net-new eval runner section.
findings.finding: The `rebar criteria eval <id>` calibration/agreement view output schema is never defined: the AC names four metrics (recall, false-accept, Cohen's κ, agreement %) but provides no rendering format, field names, or output shape for the new CLI command's printed output — an executor cannot implement the display without inventing the schema.
findings.location: § Net-new / Per-criterion eval runner; AC: `rebar criteria eval <id>` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC or § Net-new: 'The CLI prints one row per criterion in the format: `{criterion_id}: recall={r:.2f} false_accept={fa:.2f} kappa={k:.3f} agreement={a:.1f}%` (or equivalent JSON with those field names)'; specify whether a machine-readable (`--json`) flag is required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the `rebar.llm.criteria` unification AC into a clearly-labelled follow-on AC or child epic entry (e.g., prefix it with '[Post-b744]' and exclude it from this plan's definition-of-done), so this plan has a self-contained, in-session-completable AC set.
findings.finding: The AC for the shared `rebar.llm.criteria` unification layer is explicitly blocked on `b744` (a separate in-flight epic), making it not in-session completable as part of this plan's deliverables — yet it is listed as a first-class AC without being deferred or scoped out.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A shared `rebar.llm.criteria` layer' AC
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two ACs: (1) THIS plan: 'plan-review consumes `rebar.llm.criteria`; code-review still uses its own registry stub'; (2) follow-on: 'code-review migrated to `rebar.llm.criteria` after b744 lands.' Mark (2) as out-of-scope for this plan's sign-off.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the deploy/restart protocol for the long-lived MCP server when a new rebar binary is released (e.g., whether a process restart is required, or whether an in-process `_invalidate_caches()` call can be triggered via MCP), so the cache-isolation guarantee extends to binary upgrades, not just cross-repo calls.
findings.finding: The plan addresses cross-repo cache leakage in the long-lived MCP server but does not specify the deploy/restart protocol when a new rebar binary is released — there is no stated deploy ordering or restart trigger that flushes the `@lru_cache`d `_routing_index()` on an already-running server instance.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'Caching (clears G6 cache bug)' + 'A RED test must prove no cross-repo routing leakage in the long-lived MCP server'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Migration section: 'Deploying a new binary requires an MCP server process restart to flush `_routing_index()`; alternatively, expose `_invalidate_caches()` as an MCP admin command so operators can flush without restarting.' This closes the T12(c) deploy-ordering gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align ef7e and 5065 on a single ADR lifecycle: ef7e creates the ADR stub (overlay/activation/cache contract); 5065 extends it (code-review unification, cross-gate attestation) with an explicit 'extends ADR-0015' note — define the handoff in both children.
findings.finding: Child 5065 declares it will produce 'An ADR + CLAUDE.md/docs/ how-to documents the unified registry across gates,' while child ef7e already commits to creating docs/adr/0015-project-supplied-criteria.md documenting the overlay merge/activation contract. Both stories target overlapping ADR content with no explicit handoff (extend vs. replace vs. co-author) defined.
findings.location: child ef7e-c3ed-296b-4642 (AC6 + remediation ADR section) and child 5065-0192-cc5e-4d01 (AC7)
findings.suggested_fix: In ef7e, scope the ADR to plan-review overlay/activation/cache (the MVP deliverable) and add a TODO section for cross-gate unification. In 5065, explicitly reference and extend ADR-0015 rather than creating a new one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism for user value: either (a) a dogfooding target (rebar's own repo activates ≥1 project criterion end-to-end as a system test), (b) a before/after workflow comparison for a representative project maintainer task, or (c) a measurable adoption/onboarding metric (e.g., time-to-first-activation) included in the docs AC.
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism ties the feature's success to an observable improvement for the named stakeholder. The ACs cover functional correctness (cache isolation, RED tests, namespace enforcement) and the eval CLI covers LLM calibration, but there is no before/after workflow comparison, operational metric target, or dogfooding scenario demonstrating that a client project maintainer can successfully author, activate, and act on a project criterion in a realistic workflow.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Success Criteria: 'rebar's own `.rebar/` overlay activates at least one project criterion (DET or LLM) as a living end-to-end regression of the authoring→activate→run→surface→attest path.' This serves as dogfooding and provides a concrete before/after signal (overlay absent vs. overlay present, criterion fires in CI).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete sanitization rule for converting criterion ids to workflow output keys (e.g., 'replace every character not in `[a-zA-Z0-9_]` with `_`; collapse consecutive underscores; assert uniqueness after sanitization — error on collision').
findings.finding: The sanitization rule for converting a `project.<name>` criterion id to a valid workflow output key is only partially specified by example (`.`→`_`), leaving the handling of other disallowed characters (hyphens, slashes, spaces, etc.) undefined.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — Discovery bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace all characters outside `[a-zA-Z0-9_]` with `_` and assert that the resulting sanitized ids are unique within the active set (raise a load-time error on collision after sanitization).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the DET plan-review scan scope with the no-grandfathering claim: either (a) specify that DET invariants at plan-review time scan only the plan text (not repo source), (b) restrict plan-review DET to a defined subset of the repo (e.g., files mentioned in the plan), or (c) acknowledge that grandfathering/baseline IS needed for plan-review DET and add a covering AC.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'no baseline/grandfathering is needed' because 'matches are gate-scoped (diff / plan)' — but at plan-review time there is no diff, so a DET pattern-rule scan via engine_b.scan runs against the whole-repo content. A pre-existing architectural violation (e.g., a banned import already in the codebase) would surface as a DET match at plan-review time regardless of whether the plan introduces it. This directly contradicts the stated rationale that gate-scoping eliminates the need for grandfathering.
findings.location: § DET-invariant scan scoping & fail-open — paragraph starting 'Because matches are gate-scoped'
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify that at plan-review time, a DET criterion's scope is limited to artefacts the plan explicitly declares intent to introduce/modify (e.g., file paths mentioned in the plan body), not a full repo scan — making it structurally impossible to surface pre-existing violations. Update the rationale accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion for the routing derivation/validation pipeline: specify what 'no generator/drift gate' means in testable terms (e.g., a lint/CI check that validates criteria_routing.json against the schema, or explicitly descope it with a rationale).
findings.finding: The 'Routing derivation/validation pipeline' is listed as a net-new deliverable in the 'What exists vs. what we build' section ('criteria_routing.json is hand-maintained; no generator/drift gate') but no Acceptance Criterion covers it. This is an orphan deliverable — described work with no verifiable done-condition.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — Net-new bullet: 'Routing derivation/validation pipeline'
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an AC such as 'A CI validation step rejects a malformed or schema-violating .rebar/criteria_routing.json at load time (distinct from the collision/rebind check in AC#2)' or explicitly mark the routing pipeline as out-of-scope in this epic with a follow-on ticket reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC#3 (cache isolation) or AC#1 to explicitly cover `effective_routing(repo_root)`: specify that it is uncached (or keyed by repo_root + overlay mtime) and that the RED test exercises routing leakage through this function, not only through effective_criteria.
findings.finding: The caching subsection introduces `effective_routing(repo_root)` as a distinct, named function alongside `effective_criteria(repo_root)`, but no Acceptance Criterion covers `effective_routing` — only `effective_criteria` appears in AC#1. The relationship between the two (whether `effective_criteria` calls `effective_routing` internally, or they are independent) is only implied, leaving the implementation contract for `effective_routing` unchecked.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — Caching subsection: 'add effective_routing(repo_root) (uncached, or keyed by (repo_root, overlay mtime))'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC#3 to read: 'Overlay merge is cache-isolated per repo for both effective_criteria and effective_routing (packaged index stays @lru_cache'd; overlay merge via effective_routing is not); a RED test proves no cross-repo routing leakage through either entry point in a long-lived process.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) bound the `project.<name>` namespace to a safe identifier grammar (e.g. `[a-z0-9_]+`) enforced at load-time collision-check, or (b) add a probe/test that exercises the workflow output-key generation with names containing `.`, `-`, and other plausible characters and asserts all produce valid, unique keys.
findings.finding: The workflow output-key sanitization rule (`.`→`_` for `project.<name>` ids) is asserted without a probe against the actual workflow framework's key constraints. If `project.<name>` can contain characters beyond `.` that are invalid in workflow output keys (e.g. `-`, spaces, or unicode), the single substitution rule silently produces invalid keys at workflow-definition time — potentially dropping an activated criterion from the fan-out entirely with no error.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'the `include_<id>` output key must sanitize the `project.<name>` id, e.g. `.`→`_`, to a valid workflow output key'
findings.suggested_fix: Restrict `<name>` in `project.<name>` to `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+` and enforce this at load time (alongside the no-rebind check). Then the sanitization rule is `[^a-zA-Z0-9_]`→`_` with a uniqueness assert, and a single unit test covers all cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an Acceptance Criterion for the routing derivation/validation pipeline (with an observable proof, e.g., 'a drift gate fails CI if `criteria_routing.json` diverges from source-of-truth') or explicitly move it to a deferred/out-of-scope item so it is not listed as a net-new build without a corresponding AC.
findings.finding: The 'Routing derivation/validation pipeline' is listed as a net-new deliverable in 'What exists vs. what we build' but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion, no build-order step, and no observable end-state proof — it is neither clearly in-scope (provable) nor clearly deferred.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Net-new (build)' last bullet: 'Routing derivation/validation pipeline (`criteria_routing.json` is hand-maintained; no generator/drift gate)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete mechanism by which an activated project criterion's `include_project_<name>` output key enters the workflow batch step's `when` predicate set — either (a) `plan_review_assemble_criteria` emits the sanitized key and the batch step is made dynamically extensible (how?), or (b) project criteria take a different execution path (a separate scripted op or an extension slot in the batch step), rather than assuming the static `canonical` list and the static batch `when` predicates can be extended without a workflow YAML change.
findings.finding: The plan says project criteria ids must enter the 'workflow Pass-1 batch vocabulary' with sanitized output keys, but the batch step's `when` predicates are static workflow-document-time expressions — the YAML `plan_review_assemble_criteria` op builds `include_<ID>` output keys from a hardcoded `canonical = sorted(set(registry.CANONICAL_LLM) - {'ISF'})` list (workflow_ops.py:154). A project criterion added to `effective_criteria()` will never receive an `include_project_<name>` output key unless the batch step's `when` predicates are also updated. The plan acknowledges the sanitization of `.`→`_` but does not state HOW the batch step's `when` predicates (authored at workflow-YAML-write time) are extended to cover dynamically-discovered project criteria. Without a stated mechanism, an activated project criterion will still never fan out in the Pass-1 batch even after `effective_criteria()` returns it.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'the workflow Pass-1 batch vocabulary (workflow_ops builds the include_<ID> fan-out from the canonical set at workflow-definition time — project ids must enter it too, else an activated criterion never fans out)'; workflow_ops.py:154-165
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-blast-radius path: extend `plan_review_assemble_criteria` to also emit `include_project_<name>` keys for activated project criteria (from `effective_criteria(repo_root)`), AND add a companion catch-all batch slot in the workflow YAML that iterates over all `include_project_*=true` keys (e.g. a `map`-style step rather than per-criterion `when` predicates). Alternatively, state that project criteria run through a dedicated second batch step after the canonical batch, with its own project-criteria-only `include_project_<name>` predicates generated dynamically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to the `POST /criterion/preview` synchronous endpoint, document why the existing Inspect-AI eval runner / `eval_solver.run_case` pattern is not reused directly for editor preview, and explicitly resolve the spike gate question (sync vs async) before implementation begins rather than leaving it as a mid-implementation pivot.
findings.finding: The `POST /criterion/preview` synchronous endpoint is introduced as the mechanism for 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' — a new endpoint that runs a criterion live against a fixture using the eval runner. The plan notes a 'Spike gate: if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout on the reference model, fall back to an async job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's ThreadingHTTPServer handler.' The plan does not document consideration of the existing Inspect-AI eval seam and `eval_solver.run_case` as a potential basis for this capability, nor does it explain why a new endpoint is needed rather than reusing/extending the existing CLI eval path (`rebar criteria eval`) it also introduces in the same section. The async-fallback 'spike gate' implies the synchronous approach may be architecturally incorrect for ThreadingHTTPServer handlers — a risk that established async-eval patterns (job+poll) already solve.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism by which a newly activated `project.<name>` criterion enters the static `plan-review.yaml` batch criteria list — options include: (a) regenerate/rewrite the YAML `batch: criteria:` section at activation time via a CLI command (like the existing `criteria_routing.json` derivation), (b) make the batch runner dynamically compute membership from `effective_criteria(repo_root)` instead of reading the static YAML list, or (c) add a dedicated 'project criteria' batch step that runs after the canonical step.
findings.finding: The `plan-review.yaml` workflow YAML has a statically enumerated `batch: criteria:` list (31 hard-coded `prompt: plan-review-<ID>` / `when: ${{ steps.assemble.outputs.include_<ID> }}` entries). The plan says project criteria 'must enter [the `include_<id>` fan-out]' and that '`workflow_ops` builds the `include_<ID>` fan-out from the canonical set at workflow-definition time', but does not specify a concrete mechanism for extending the static YAML batch list. Today `plan_review_assemble_criteria` emits `include_<cid>` only for `sorted(registry.CANONICAL_LLM)` (workflow_ops.py:154-165), and the YAML batch criteria list is a committed artifact. There is no existing dynamic-extension hook for the batch criteria list.
findings.location: § Discovery — 'the workflow Pass-1 batch vocabulary (`workflow_ops` builds the `include_<ID>` fan-out from the canonical set at workflow-definition time — project ids must enter it too)'; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml:55-117` (static batch criteria list); `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:154-165` (canonical-only fan-out)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete extension mechanism for the per-criterion eval runner: either (a) a new `run_criterion_case(criterion_id, case, runner)` function separate from `eval_solver.run_case`, or (b) a documented branch added to `run_case` — with the invocation path (how a criterion id maps to a plan-review finder call) made explicit.
findings.finding: The plan says to extend `eval_solver.run_case` for the per-criterion eval runner, but the existing function is a hard-coded 3-case dispatch (`completion-verifier`, `ticket-quality`, `spec-alignment`) that raises `ValueError` on any other `prompt_id` — 'extending' it to handle arbitrary criterion ids requires structural surgery (not a simple additive branch), and the extension mechanism is not described.
findings.location: § Net-new / Per-criterion eval runner; § Build order step 3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'extending the closed solver dispatch' with: 'Add `run_criterion_case(criterion_id, case, *, runner)` to `eval_solver.py` that resolves the criterion descriptor via `registry.by_id()[criterion_id]`, runs it as a Pass-1 finder against a fixture plan seeded from `case["plan"]`, and returns the finder's `{findings: [...]}` output — separate from the existing `run_case` dispatch which handles whole-op reviewers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to child 08af (or ef7e) asserting that a blocking project criterion is bypassable via --force, with a test confirming this; or explicitly document that the existing --force path is exercised by the same kernel Pass-3 path project criteria use.
findings.finding: No child's acceptance criteria explicitly tests or asserts that a blocking project criterion (DET or LLM) is escapable via --force. The parent AC states 'A project criterion may block (project-configured threshold/posture), --force-escapable' but 08af explicitly says 'Blocking itself is NOT in scope (already shipped)' and provides no --force AC for project criteria. The existing --force mechanism covers built-ins; no child verifies it generalizes to project criteria.
findings.location: child 08af-b2f0-aef0-4d59 (Scope section and ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: In 08af, add: '[ ] A test confirms that a plan-review run with --force passes even when an activated blocking project criterion would otherwise block; the test covers both a DET and an LLM project criterion configured to block.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
